blog.hero_sub: Updates, news and stories from the heart of the Waterberg.

posts.title: New cheetah cubs born at Nova's Legacy
posts.excerpt: Great joy in the reserve: our female Amira has given birth to three healthy cubs. The veterinary team is monitoring them 24 hours a day. Updates to follow.
body: On the morning of 12 May 2025, at around 5:30 am, Kim noticed the first signs of Amira's labour. Three hours later, three cubs — two males and one female — opened their eyes to the world for the first time.

The birth of cheetah cubs is always a delicate moment. Cubs weigh between 200 and 300 grams at birth and in the first days depend entirely on their mother for warmth and nourishment. Our veterinary team is present 24 hours a day to monitor vital signs and intervene if necessary.

Amira is an attentive and protective mother. In the days following the birth she has already shown a very strong maternal instinct, regularly nursing all three cubs. Infant mortality in cheetahs is unfortunately high in the wild — between 50% and 70% in the first year — but in a controlled environment like ours, survival rates increase significantly.
Follow our social channels for weekly updates on Amira and her cubs. We will also soon announce the names — and you can take part in choosing them!

posts.title: Testimonial: six weeks in the bush with the cheetahs
posts.excerpt: Marco, 24, a biology student from Turin, tells us about his volunteering experience. "I learned things I will never find in a textbook."
body: "I arrived with a rucksack full of expectations and some fear. First morning, up at 6, straight to work: cleaning the enclosures, preparing food, behavioural observation. There was no time for anxiety."

Marco spent six weeks at Nova's Legacy as part of his master's degree in conservation biology. During his stay he assisted Kim with veterinary visits, contributed to data collection on cheetah behaviour and participated in the environmental education programme for local schools.

"The thing that struck me most wasn't the cheetahs — even though running alongside Nova is an experience I'll carry forever. It was the humanity of the place. Kim knows every animal by name, knows their history, habits, character. It's not a reserve, it's a family."

Marco will return to Nova's Legacy in autumn to complete his data collection for his thesis. In the meantime, we recommend his account to anyone considering a volunteering experience: his words describe better than any brochure what it truly means to be part of the Coalition.

posts.title: Why the cheetah is more at risk than the lion
posts.excerpt: An in-depth look at the factors that make the cheetah particularly vulnerable: from low genetic diversity to conflict with farmers.
body: The lion is the symbol of Africa, yet it is the cheetah — with fewer than 7,000 individuals remaining — that is classified as Vulnerable by the IUCN. Why?

The first factor is genetic. Cheetahs went through one or more evolutionary bottlenecks around 10,000 years ago, drastically reducing the species' genetic diversity. This makes them particularly vulnerable to disease and environmental change: two genetically similar individuals respond in the same way to the same pathogen.

The second factor is territorial. Unlike lions and leopards, cheetahs need large open spaces to hunt and live at very low densities. Agricultural expansion has reduced and fragmented their natural habitat, forcing them increasingly into inhabited areas and farmland.

Conflict with farmers is the third major problem. A cheetah that kills goats or calves is often eliminated by the landowner — and since cheetahs live 77% of the time outside protected areas, this type of conflict is very common. Nova's Legacy actively works with local communities to develop coexistence strategies that protect both the animals and the livelihoods of local families.

posts.title: Spirit finds a home: the story of a rescued horse
posts.excerpt: Spirit arrived with us in poor condition. Six months later, he trots freely in the reserve. The story of his rehabilitation.
body: Spirit arrived at Nova's Legacy in July 2024, referred by a local vet. He was underweight, had hoof problems and clear signs of neglect. Kim welcomed him without hesitation.

The first weeks were the hardest. Spirit was distrustful of humans and showed anxious behaviours typical of an animal with a difficult history. The team worked patiently, giving him his own time, offering quality food and regular veterinary care.
The turning point came after about a month. Spirit began spontaneously approaching volunteers during morning feeding. From that moment, his trust grew quickly.

Today Spirit weighs 480 kg, has a shiny coat and healthy hooves. Every morning he trots the perimeter of the reserve, as if checking that everything is in its place. He has become a favourite with the children who visit the reserve on school trips — and perhaps one of the most effective ambassadors for our rehabilitation programme.

posts.title: 200 children visit from the Bela-Bela school
posts.excerpt: Our educational programme welcomed 200 local students. For many of them, it was the first time they had ever seen a cheetah up close.
body: On 20 January 2025, Nova's Legacy opened its gates to 200 primary school students from Bela-Bela, aged 7 to 12. It was the largest educational day in our history.

The programme included four stations: an introduction to cheetah biology given by Kim, a bush tracking demonstration, observation of the cheetahs in the enclosure and a practical workshop on how to build a wildlife shelter using natural materials.

For many children — particularly those who grew up in urban areas — it was the first time they had been within a few metres of a large cat. Reactions always vary: some approach with curiosity, some stop at a safe distance with wide eyes, some fire questions that even test Kim.

"Education is the foundation of every conservation project," says Kim. "If these children grow up with a real understanding of what the cheetah is — not the stylised image on a T-shirt, but the real animal, with its needs and its fragility — then the future of the species is in better hands."

posts.title: The Cheetah Run: how it works and how to prepare
posts.excerpt: Everything you need to know before running alongside the fastest cheetah in the world. Tips, FAQ and booking.
body: The Cheetah Run is the most thrilling experience we offer at Nova's Legacy. For 60 metres, you run alongside a cheetah at full speed. It lasts about 8 seconds. People talk about it for years.

How it works: arrive at the reserve at least 30 minutes before your booked time. Kim or one of the trainers explains the safety rules and introduces you to the cheetah you'll be running with. The track is flat, bounded by a low fence on the cheetah's side. On the signal, you both go. The cheetah is guided by a visual lure and an experienced trainer.

What to expect physically: the cheetah starts slow, then accelerates. Even at reduced speed — it never reaches 112 km/h in this setup — it's an intense sensory experience. The sound of paws on the dry ground, the dust thrown up, the feeling of running alongside something much, much faster than you.

Practical tips: wear closed running shoes and comfortable clothing. Bring water. Don't wear strong perfume. If you have knee or back problems, check with us before booking. Minimum age: 12. No athletic preparation required — the cheetah sets the pace.

animals.name: Storm
animals.bio: Storm came to us malnourished and frightened, rescued from a facility that couldn't give her the care she deserved. Years of patient love later, she's a calm, content golden tabby tiger who shares her enclosure with her best friend Quake.

animals.name: Guinness
animals.bio: Born with severe breathing problems that needed round-the-clock care, Guinness pulled through to become one of the sweetest cheetahs on the reserve — a gentle purring machine with the rare, striped coat of a king cheetah.

animals.name: Lyvia
animals.bio: Lyvia survived a black mamba attack as a six-day-old cub that tragically took her mother's life, and pulled through emergency surgery against the odds. Today she's healthy, affectionate, and loves lying close to the people she trusts.

animals.name: Oakley
animals.bio: Oakley overcame a serious intestinal blockage that required emergency surgery as a cub. Now fully recovered, she's known on the reserve for her grumpy little face — and for being one of our most resilient success stories.

animals.name: Jagger
animals.bio: Jagger was just two months old when an x-ray revealed multiple fractures from a difficult start in life. Hand-raised back to health, he's now a sweet, playful cheetah with a soft spot for chewing on red toys.

footer.brand_desc: A cheetah breeding and conservation project in the heart of the Waterberg, South Africa. Founded by Kim Hiltrop — a veterinary nurse who hand-raised Nova, a three-legged cheetah.
footer.copyright: © 2026 Nova's Legacy · Feracare Wildlife Centre · Bela-Bela, Limpopo

home.hero_sub: Nova's Legacy is a cheetah breeding and conservation centre in the heart of the Waterberg. Founded by Kim Hiltrop for Nova — a three-legged cheetah hand-raised from birth.
home.work_title: Three pillars to save a species
home.work_desc: For over 11 years we have been working on three fronts: responsible breeding, environmental education and animal rehabilitation. Every programme is designed to maximise the impact on cheetah survival.
home.pillar1_desc: A certified breeding programme contributing to the global gene pool. We collaborate with international reserves and zoos.
home.pillar2_desc: We bring local and international students to learn about wildlife. Every visit is a seed planted for the future.
home.pillar3_desc: We welcome injured or orphaned animals — cheetahs, horses, wild dogs — and care for them to return them to nature.
home.run_title: The Cheetah Run — running alongside the king of speed
home.run_desc: The cheetah is the fastest land animal on the planet. With our Cheetah Run you have the opportunity to run alongside one on a 60-metre track, feel the thunder of its strides, watch it accelerate to 112 km/h.
home.run_speed_label: km/h top speed of the cheetah

home.vol_title: Live the bush. Save a species.
home.vol_desc1: Volunteering at Nova's Legacy is not a tourist experience — it is real work. You will contribute to the daily care of the animals, the maintenance of the reserve, and the education of visitors.
home.vol_desc2: In return: accommodation in the bush, three communal meals a day, a passionate team and the satisfaction of doing something that truly matters.
home.animals_desc: Everybody is welcome at Nova's Legacy. Discover the stories of all the animals present at the camp.

home.cta_desc: Donate, adopt, volunteer or simply share. Every gesture counts. Every voice matters.

testi_quotes: An experience that changed my perspective on life. Seeing Nova run free is something I will never forget.
Kim and her team are incredible. I learned more in three weeks here than in a year of university.
The chalets are comfortable, the food excellent. But the best thing is waking up in contact with these extraordinary animals.
accom_descs: Self-catering, fully equipped. Kitchen, private bathroom, double or twin bed.
A few steps from the animal enclosures. At night you can hear the cheetahs.
Every morning before heading to the animals. Lunch and dinner on request.
Hot water, electricity. Everything you need, nothing superfluous.
home.alert_body_2: still live in the wild. In the last 100 years we have lost
home.alert_body_3: of them. Without intervention, this species will not survive.

cheetah.hero_sub: Responsible breeding, genetic heritage conservation and a unique experience to discover the fastest feline in the world.
cheetah.nova_p1: Nova was a three-legged cheetah, rescued from a desperate situation and hand-raised by Kim Hiltrop — a veterinary nurse with a heart as big as Africa. Nova could not survive in the wild, but she gave birth to something extraordinary: an entire conservation project that bears her name.
cheetah.nova_p2: Today Nova's Legacy is much more than a sanctuary. It is a certified breeding centre, a reference point for cheetah conservation in the Waterberg, and a home for dozens of wild animals.
cheetah.highlight: The cheetah is the feline species most at risk in Africa. Fewer than 7,000 individuals still live in the wild — 80% of the decline has occurred in the last 100 years. Poaching, habitat loss and conflict with farmers are the main causes.
cheetah.run_p1: The Cheetah Run is the most adrenaline-filled experience you can have at Nova's Legacy. Run on a 60-metre track alongside a live cheetah, feel its explosive acceleration, watch it reach 112 km/h. This is not a show — it is a window into nature that changes you forever.
cheetah.run_p2: Available for visitors, groups and volunteers. The experience lasts approximately 15–20 minutes including the safety briefing. Contact us by email or WhatsApp to book.
cheetah.breeding_p1: Our breeding programme is developed in collaboration with international conservation experts. Every pairing is planned to maximise genetic diversity in the captive population, reducing inbreeding and increasing the vigour of individuals.
cheetah.breeding_p2: Cubs born at Nova's Legacy are monitored from their first days. Where possible, procedures are initiated for reintroduction to the wild through rewilding programmes in collaboration with partner reserves.

conservation.hero_sub: We work every day to change the narrative around the cheetah — from feared predators to ambassadors of a healthy ecosystem.
conservation.mission_p: Nova's Legacy operates with a clear mandate: to prevent the extinction of the cheetah in South Africa through responsible breeding, education of local communities and scientific research. We are registered as a non-profit organisation (PBO No. 930069839) and collaborate with national and international institutions.
conservation.highlight: In a hundred years we have lost 90% of the world's cheetahs. Fewer than 7,000 survive in the wild. South Africa is home to approximately 1,300 individuals — one of the highest numbers on the continent, but still critical.
conservation.edu_p1: Nova's Legacy's educational programme welcomes school groups, universities and tour operators for guided sessions in the reserve. Every visit includes a tour of the enclosures, a close encounter with the animals (where safe) and a presentation on cheetah biology and conservation.
conservation.edu_p2: We believe that education is the true antidote to extinction: a child who has seen a cheetah up close never forgets it. And as an adult, they become its guardian.
conservation.science_p: We collaborate with university students and international researchers to collect behavioural, genetic and health data on our animals. The data collected is shared with the scientific community to contribute to the understanding of cheetah biology in captivity and in the wild.
conservation.coexist_p: One of the main reasons for the cheetah's decline is conflict with local farmers. Cheetahs kill livestock — and farmers kill them in response. We work with Waterberg communities to find coexistence solutions: guard dogs, electric fencing, compensation for losses.

horses.hero_sub: A second chance for horses rescued from difficult situations. Rehabilitation, care and human connection.
horses.who_p1: Nova's Legacy's horse project was born from the same philosophy that guides all our work: every animal deserves respect, care and the opportunity to live a dignified life.
horses.who_p2: The horses we host come from situations of abandonment, mistreatment or inability to be cared for. They are assessed by veterinarians, treated for any physical issues and gradually rehabilitated through a relational re-education programme.
horses.highlight: Equine rehabilitation is a slow process that requires patience, consistency and respect. Volunteers actively participate in this journey — while also learning the basics of riding and non-verbal communication.
horses.vol_p1: Volunteers who choose to work with the horses follow a specific programme that includes: cleaning and maintenance of the stables, feeding and supplements, grooming sessions, lunge work and, for those with experience, supervised riding sessions.
horses.vol_p2: No previous experience with horses is required. The team teaches everything from the beginning, in complete safety. What matters is the right attitude: calm, respect and openness to learning.
horses.ecosystem_p1: Nova's Legacy's horses live in the same reserve as cheetahs, wild dogs, giraffes and zebras. This creates an extraordinarily rich environment, where every morning is a different discovery.
horses.ecosystem_p2: Many volunteers say the most memorable moment of their stay was seeing a cheetah and a horse separated only by the fence, looking at each other with mutual curiosity.

volunteer.hero_sub: Not a holiday with animals. Real work, alongside a passionate team, in one of the most beautiful places in South Africa.
volunteer.what_title: What it means to volunteer here
volunteer.what_p1: Volunteering at Nova's Legacy is not a tourist experience. It is real work: you will rise early, work hard, get your hands dirty and fall asleep to the sounds of the African bush. Every day you will have a direct impact on the lives of the animals we care for.
volunteer.what_p2: In return you receive: accommodation in the bush (chalet or tent, subject to availability), three communal meals a day, mentoring from the professional team and the satisfaction of doing something that truly matters.
volunteer.highlight: Minimum duration: 2 weeks. The ideal time is 4–8 weeks — enough to become part of the team and see real progress in the animals. Longer stay? Contact us, we'll find a solution.
volunteer.apply_text: Write us an email at kim@novaslegacy.co.za or send a WhatsApp message to +27 82 352 0940. Introduce yourself, tell us about your interests and give us your preferred dates. We will reply with availability, prices and a complete information pack.

schedule.title: Bush Sunrise
schedule.desc: Wake up to the sounds of the savanna. Prepare food and enrichments for all the animals.

schedule.title: Feeding
schedule.desc: Feed the cheetahs, wild dogs, caracals and all the reserve's residents. Learn their personalities.

schedule.title: Enclosure Work
schedule.desc: Maintenance, cleaning and habitat improvement. Physical work with a clear purpose.

schedule.title: Lunch & Rest
schedule.desc: Shared meal with views of the Waterberg mountains. A moment of community.

schedule.title: Afternoon Activities
schedule.desc: Educational sessions, enrichments, horse project, or game drives in the reserve.

schedule.title: Evening Meal & Sunset
schedule.desc: Final rounds, dinner together, and the most spectacular sunsets you have ever seen.

tasks: Daily care and feeding of the animals
Cleaning and maintenance of the enclosures

internship.hero_sub: Hands-on conservation experience for students of veterinary medicine, zoology, ecology and wildlife management.
internship.learn_p1: The internship at Nova's Legacy is designed for university students who want to turn theory into real practice. No simulations, no passive observation — you work directly with the animals, under the supervision of experienced professionals.
internship.learn_p2: Each intern follows a personalised programme based on their academic background and training objectives agreed with their university. We provide official documentation for the recognition of academic credits.
internship.highlight: Nova's Legacy collaborates with South African, European and American universities. If your university does not yet have a formal agreement with us, contact us: we work to find a solution on a case-by-case basis.
internship.includes_p: The internship includes: accommodation in the reserve (chalet or shared), three meals a day, access to all areas of the reserve, participation in operational meetings, support in data collection for theses or research, direct mentoring from Kim Hiltrop and the veterinary team.
internship.duration_p: Minimum duration: 4 weeks. Recommended: 8–12 weeks. The programme cost covers accommodation, meals and supervision. Write to us to receive a detailed quote based on the chosen duration.

visit.hero_sub: Three self-catering chalets immersed in the Waterberg. Breakfast included, wild animals within sight, and a silence you will not find anywhere else in the world.
visit.chalets_p: Our self-catering chalets are designed for those who want to experience authentic Africa without sacrificing comfort. Each chalet features a fully equipped kitchen, private bathroom, hot water and WiFi. Breakfast is included in the price every morning.
visit.how_p1: We are approximately 130 km north of Pretoria, in the Bela-Bela (Warmbaths) district, Limpopo. The exact address is: 431 Diepdrift, Bela-Bela, 0480, South Africa.
visit.how_p2: The nearest airport is OR Tambo (Johannesburg), approximately 2 hours' drive away. We can arrange a transfer on request (additional cost). For directions once in the area, we'll send you precise instructions at the time of booking.
visit.highlight: Recommended periods: April–October (dry season, ideal temperature, animals more visible). Summer (Nov–Mar) is hot and rainy but equally magical. The Waterberg is a year-round destination.
visit.add_run_p: All chalet guests can book the Cheetah Run during their stay. It is the experience that turns a holiday into a lifelong memory. Available on weekdays, morning or afternoon.

chalets.name: Chalet Nova
chalets.desc: The main chalet, dedicated to our founder. Views of the cheetah enclosures. Double bedroom, private bathroom, equipped kitchen.

chalets.name: Chalet Bush
chalets.desc: Immersed in the Waterberg vegetation. Twin or double beds, private outdoor space, braai (African barbecue) included.

chalets.name: Chalet Waterberg
chalets.desc: The most panoramic. Terrace with mountain views, perfect for families or couples seeking absolute privacy in nature.

adopt.hero_sub: Can't come in person? You can still make a difference. Symbolically adopt one of our animals and receive direct updates.
adopt.desc: With a symbolic adoption you receive: an official certificate with photo, monthly updates directly from Kim about the animal's life, and the knowledge that your contribution goes 100% towards food and medical care.
adopt.highlight: To adopt, write to kim@novaslegacy.co.za indicating the animal chosen. We will reply within 48 hours with payment instructions and your personalised adoption certificate.
adopt.what_you_get_text: personalised digital certificate ◆ monthly photo and video updates ◆ priority access to reserve news ◆ option to dedicate the adoption as a gift
adopt.portal_desc: Enter the email used at the time of adoption to access the portal where you can update your payment card, change the amount or cancel at any time.
adopt.portal_error_not_found: No subscription found for this email.

info.desc: 100% of proceeds → animal care and conservation.

nova_story.hero_sub: Born from a responsible mating between unrelated cheetahs, Nova became the symbol of a mission that goes far beyond a single life.
nova_story.origins_p1: Nova was not born by chance. She is the result of a scientifically planned union between two cheetahs with no blood relationship — a deliberate choice, based on genetic analysis, with a clear objective: to preserve what remains of the genetic diversity of an already severely compromised species.
nova_story.origins_p2: Her parents were selected from available individuals precisely because they shared no common line of descent. This type of pairing — called outbreeding — is the fundamental practice of Nova's Legacy for combating inbreeding in captive cheetahs.
nova_story.highlight: Nova is proof that the method works. Healthy, lively, with a strong immune system and extraordinary vitality — every characteristic that defines her is the result of that genetic choice made before she was even born.
nova_story.genetics_p1: About 10,000 years ago, a catastrophic event — probably an ice age — reduced the entire world population of cheetahs to very few individuals. From that genetic "bottleneck," all living cheetahs today are descended. The result: 99% of their DNA is identical. Siblings and unrelated individuals are almost indistinguishable at a genetic level.
nova_story.genetics_p2: The practical consequences are severe: a weakened immune system, reduced male fertility (over 70% of sperm have abnormal morphology), high vulnerability to disease and reduced resistance to environmental stress. Mating individuals who are already genetically similar makes this situation even worse.
nova_story.genetics_p3: This is why at Nova's Legacy every mating is carefully planned. Only individuals with no blood relationship are chosen, often in coordination with international reserves and zoos participating in the same genetic conservation programme.
nova_story.legacy_p1: The reserve bears her name — not out of nostalgia, but out of commitment. Nova represents what this place wants to be every day: somewhere science and care meet, where every birth is a small but real victory against extinction.
nova_story.legacy_p2: Today Nova lives in her area of the reserve, observed and loved by hundreds of visitors every year. And every cub born at Nova's Legacy carries the same principle that brought her into the world: parents chosen for their genetic distance, no blood relationship, maximum possible diversity. It is her legacy.

cheetah_run.hero_sub: Run alongside the fastest cheetah in the world. An experience that lasts a few seconds but stays with you forever.
cheetah_run.title: The Cheetah Run
cheetah_run.p1: The Cheetah Run is the most adrenaline-filled experience you can have at Nova's Legacy. Run on a 60-metre track alongside a live cheetah, feel its explosive acceleration, watch it reach 112 km/h. This is not a show — it is a window into nature that changes you forever.
cheetah_run.highlight: The cheetah is the fastest land animal on the planet. In just three seconds it accelerates from 0 to 96 km/h. Its paws touch the ground for only half the time during the run — for the other half, it literally flies.
cheetah_run.how_p1: The experience takes place in a dedicated area within the reserve. An adult cheetah, accustomed to human presence, is brought to the starting zone. At the signal, the animal begins to run following a visual stimulus — and you run with it. The distance is 60 metres: enough to feel the wind, enough to never forget.
cheetah_run.how_p2: Available for visitors, groups and volunteers. Total duration approximately 15–20 minutes including the safety briefing.
cheetah_run.practical_p: Wear running shoes and comfortable clothing. Bring a camera — but make sure it is secure with a strap or in a pouch. Minimum age: 12 years. No particular athletic experience required: what matters is being fit for a short, intense run.
cheetah_run.booking_highlight: To book write to kim@novaslegacy.co.za or call +27 82 352 0940. Available every day, morning and afternoon, by appointment. We recommend booking at least 48 hours in advance.

donate.hero_sub: Your donation directly funds the care of the animals and the conservation programme.
donate.success_desc: Your donation will directly help the animals at Nova's Legacy.

impact.desc: Feeds a cheetah for one week

wishlist.sub_label: ~ Support our animals: join us in making a difference ~
wishlist.p1: If that is a question you have asked yourself, then this page is for you! Please read below and we will explain what is needed both urgently and long term and how you can help us to reach our goals.
wishlist.p2: Below, you'll find our Wishlist. This is a list of products we would love to add and that would make a huge difference in the way we keep the animals in our care safe and healthy or to maintain the enclosures or facilities we need to look after them.
wishlist.urgent_title: Urgent items on this list right now
wishlist.urgent_text: are rolls of shade netting, materials to extend the electric fence and extra cameras. These items are all for our breeding camp to help us keep our moms and cubs safe from the snake who targeted our cubs last year.
wishlist.disclaimer_1: Our wishlist is designed to make it easier for you to select and donate items we need instead of donating the funds directly. Some items will be brand specific (like our wifi cameras or animal supplements and medications) where other items (like tools, food, etc) does not need the specific brand we selected. If you doubt, please feel free to contact us at any time. This list is updated on a regular basis.
wishlist.disclaimer_2: We appreciate any donation, no matter how big or small!!
items: Shade netting to cover fences and provide protection. (1 roll of netting is R4200 for 50m by 3m high, we are needing 10 rolls to finish the female lanes).
Cameras to monitor and document activities (Takealot Wishlist).
Supplements specially formulated for cheetahs. (1 bag of supplements is R1200 and lasts us 1 week for all our cats).
Materials for electric fences to enhance safety and security.
Tools such as wheelbarrows, shovels, rakes, knives, and more (Takealot Wishlist).
Vaccines for cats to ensure their health.
Chicken or fresh red meat for feeding.
Game or horse feed to sustain their diets.

other_animals.hero_sub: Providing a lifelong home and sanctuary to diverse wildlife species.
other_animals.sanctuary_title: A Lifelong Home for Every Soul
other_animals.sanctuary_p1: In addition to cheetahs, we provide a lifelong home to many other animal species. Lions, tigers, and small wild cats all find the care and protection they deserve here. Alongside them, we also care for numerous free-roaming herbivores, offering them a safe place to live out their lives.
other_animals.feracare_title: In Partnership with FeraCare Wildlife Centre
other_animals.feracare_text: Our conservation efforts are strengthened by a proud collaboration with FeraCare Wildlife Centre in South Africa. FeraCare is renowned for its dedicated ethical contribution to cheetah conservation and wildlife protection. Together, we share vital research, optimize sustainable sanctuary management, and elevate care standards for rescued African fauna.
other_animals.cats_p1: From the majestic roars of our rescued lions to the elusive habits of wild cat species, our sanctuary ensures species-specific nourishment and enrichment programs. Many of our predators come from situations of neglect or human-wildlife conflict.
other_animals.small_p1: Smaller nocturnal predators and local mammals find a secure haven here. Every single habitat is custom-designed around the precise biological needs of its guests, ensuring safety, structural dignity, and specialized veterinary monitoring for those who cannot be reintegrated into the open wilderness.
other_animals.herbivores_p1: The reserve hosts vast, protected open fields where zebras, antelopes, and other free-roaming herbivores live peacefully. They play a vital role in keeping our ecosystem balanced and maintaining the natural African bushveld terrain.

kim_story.hero_sub: The woman who started it all — vet nurse, conservationist and cheetah caretaker.
kim_story.p1: Born in ’84, immediately after finishing her studies as a vet nurse in Belgium, she moved to South Africa to pursue her dream. Here begins a path of working with animals and wildlife in specific. Kim started working with birds, then moved over to small cat species, and eventually settled on big cats, gaining years of experience with species like tigers and lions. Later, while collaborating with Ann van Dyk, she specialized in raising orphaned cubs, eventually raising her first cheetah, Nova. The incredible bond she formed with him sparked her lifelong passion for cheetahs, leading her to start her own project and take over the private cheetah farm in the Waterberg area.
kim_story.quote1: Nova was the very first cheetah cub I hand reared. He taught me the beauty in cheetahs, the incredible bond they can form with their carers and all the problems they face as a species.
kim_story.quote2: Nova and I spent nearly 11 years together, a time where Nova pushed me to fight for the cheetahs' survival.
kim_story.quote3: Nova’s legacy will continue to fight for the species through captive breeding with the aim to give cheetahs a fighting chance in a world where they are running out of space.

faq.hero_sub: Find all the official details regarding volunteering, internships, accommodation on the reserve, and support channels.
faq.footer_text: If you cannot find the answer to your question, please feel free to reach out directly to Kim at

questions.q: What do volunteers do during a typical day?
questions.a: A normal working day runs from early morning to late afternoon to match our cats' active hours. Tasks begin at 8:00 AM with food preparation, followed by the feeding round at 9:00 AM. Depending on the length of your stay, duties include preparing raw game meat for carnivores, cleaning and maintaining enclosures, assisting with cheetah runs or daily training sessions, checking perimeter fences, and monitoring free-roaming game like giraffes and zebras.

questions.q: What is the weekly schedule for volunteers?
questions.a: The official working week is from Monday to Friday. Saturdays are our cats' fasting day, making it the perfect day for volunteers to book external activities or excursions in the Bela-Bela area at their own expense. Sundays are reserved for resting, though you are welcome to assist with basic daily farm chores if you wish.

questions.q: Are there any specific requirements or arrivals schedules?
questions.a: We look for dedicated, open-minded, nature and animal-loving individuals. Changeovers and airport pickups are coordinated strictly on Mondays. If your stay is complete, you will be taken to the airport on Monday morning; if you are staying longer, your new working week begins then.

questions.q: Who can apply for an internship at Nova's Legacy?
questions.a: Our internship programmes are open to university students, researchers, and educational institutions looking for a structured curriculum in wildlife management, veterinary support, and predator conservation. We work on custom dedicated plans based on your field of study.

questions.q: How does an internship differ from volunteering?
questions.a: While interns participate in daily sanctuary operations, internships focus heavily on educational research, gathering data from tracking or camera traps, giraffe identification, and deeper analytical elements of our breeding programme to preserve cheetah genetics.

questions.q: Where do volunteers and interns stay on the reserve?
questions.a: Everyone stays at our main Volunteer House or adjacent chalets. Accommodation features shared bedrooms, shared bathrooms, a common living space, and a kitchen area. There is also a garden for stargazing, a pool table, a ping-pong table, and a dedicated lapa for evening traditional braais.

questions.q: Is Wi-Fi available at the accommodation?
questions.a: Yes, the house and chalets offer free Wi-Fi. To preserve connection stability and promote team integration, internet access is accessible after working hours and throughout the entire weekend.

questions.q: How are meals handled, and can you accommodate dietary needs?
questions.a: We provide 3 basic meals a day: breakfast, a light lunch, and supper, accompanied by water, coffee, tea, or juice. The cuisine is mainly South African. If you have any specific dietary requirements or allergies, please inform us well in advance so our kitchen team can adjust.

questions.q: Is there a shop on-site, and what does it stock?
questions.a: Yes, we run an on-site shop located 60km away from the main town. The shop stocks basic snacks, carbonated soft drinks, and typical sanctuary souvenirs. Shopping trips to town are conducted once a week by our staff.

questions.q: Should I bring extra cash for my stay?
questions.a: We highly recommend bringing extra funds or 'emergency' pocket money if you are a selective eater, enjoy snacking between meals, prefer carbonated sodas, or wish to buy souvenirs from our shop during your stay.

questions.q: Can I donate physical items instead of financial funds?
questions.a: Absolutely. Our specialized Wishlist via Takealot allows supporters to select and purchase exact products we urgently need (such as shade netting rolls, fence extensions, wifi cameras, or specific predator supplements) which are then delivered directly to the sanctuary.

questions.q: How are symbolic animal adoptions and payments processed?
questions.a: All financial donations and monthly symbolic adoptions are secured safely via Stripe or PayPal. Subscriptions can be reviewed, adjusted, or cancelled at any time by requesting a direct link through our Customer Portal using your adoption email.

our_animals.hero_sub: Every species at Nova's Legacy has its own story — scroll through to meet the cheetahs, horses and wild family who call the reserve home.
our_animals.intro_p1: From the cheetahs at the heart of our mission to the horses, predators and free-roaming herbivores who share the reserve with them, every animal here has found a safe place to belong. Scroll through each species below and discover their stories.
our_animals.other_desc: Beyond cheetahs and horses, Nova's Legacy is home to a wide family of rescued wildlife and farm animals — lions, tigers, small wild cats, free-roaming herbivores and more, each with a sanctuary built around their needs.
our_animals.cheetahs_desc: Built for speed — a light frame, semi-retractable claws and a flexible spine let cheetahs reach strides of up to 7 metres. They can't roar; instead they chirp and purr.
our_animals.horses_desc: Rescued and rehabilitated horses given patient, individual care until they're ready to trust again — and to share gentle moments with our volunteers.
our_animals.lions_desc: Africa's second-largest big cat, living in close-knit prides. Maned males guard the group while lionesses do most of the hunting.
our_animals.tigers_desc: The largest cat in the world and a powerful swimmer, with fully retractable claws built for stalking silently through long grass.
our_animals.servals_desc: Tall, slender hunters with oversized ears and a leap that can clear 3 metres — perfect for snatching birds straight out of the air.
our_animals.caracals_desc: Known as the desert lynx, Africa's largest small cat can leap nearly 3 metres to knock flying birds clean out of the sky.
our_animals.civets_desc: Solitary and nocturnal, civets are recognisable by their bandit-like facial mask and use scent glands to mark their territory.
our_animals.porcupines_desc: Africa's largest porcupine species, covered in rattling quills that form a defensive crest along the head and neck when raised.
our_animals.bat_eared_foxes_desc: Small, social foxes with oversized ears tuned to hear insects moving underground, foraging together in tight family groups.
our_animals.cows_desc: Rescued cattle living out peaceful lives on the reserve, part of the wider sanctuary family we care for every day.
our_animals.pets_desc: Rescued cats and dogs who found a second chance at Nova's Legacy, living safely alongside the rest of our animal family.
our_animals.birds_desc: From birds of prey to colourful residents and migratory visitors, the reserve's skies are as alive as the bush below.
our_animals.free_roaming_animals_desc: Giraffes, zebras, antelope and other herbivores roam freely across the open reserve, keeping the bushveld ecosystem in balance.
our_animals.empty_species: No animals added for this species yet.
our_animals.jackals_desc: Highly adaptable, opportunistic canids that hunt alone or in pairs and scavenge just as readily. Monogamous for life, jackal pairs mark and defend their territory together, and are best known for their sharp, far-carrying howls used to stay in contact at dusk and through the night.
our_animals.cheetahs_detail: Built for speed, the cheetah has a light skeleton, semi-retractable claws and a long, flexible spine that lets it reach strides of up to 7 metres at full sprint. Solid black spots and dark "tear stripes" running from the eyes to the mouth help cut the sun's glare while hunting. Cheetahs can't roar — instead they chirp, purr and even churr to communicate. Males often form small coalitions for life, while females remain solitary outside of raising cubs. Fewer than 7,000 are estimated to survive in the wild today.
our_animals.horses_detail: Every horse on the reserve arrives with a different story — most rescued from neglect, abandonment or situations where they could no longer be cared for. Each is assessed by a vet, treated for any injuries or illness, and gradually rehabilitated through patient, hands-on care and a structured re-training programme. Many were once underweight or distrustful of people; today they graze freely across the reserve alongside cheetahs, wild dogs and zebras, and several have grown calm enough to share quiet moments with visiting volunteers.
our_animals.lions_detail: Africa's second-largest big cat, the lion is best known for its powerful roar and the thick mane that makes males look larger and helps protect the neck during fights. Lions are the only cat with a tufted tail, used to signal to the rest of the pride during a hunt. Cubs are born with soft spots that fade with age, and the rare white lion's pale coat comes from a genetic condition called leucism. Lions live in tight social groups called prides, with males up to 50% larger than females.
our_animals.tigers_detail: The largest cat in the world, the tiger is a powerful, surprisingly enthusiastic swimmer — one of the few big cats that actively seeks out water. Its bold stripes are unique to every individual and provide near-perfect camouflage when stalking prey through long grass, while fully retractable claws and padded paws keep its approach silent. Genetic variation produces several coat colours, from the classic orange-and-black to rare white, golden and snow-white tigers. Tigers greet one another with a friendly huffing sound called "chuffing."
our_animals.servals_detail: Tall and slender, the serval is one of the tallest small cats in Africa thanks to long legs built for leaping — it can spring up to 3 metres into the air to snatch a bird mid-flight or pounce on prey hidden in tall grass. Its oversized, mobile ears can rotate 180 degrees and pick up the ultrasonic calls of rodents moving underground. Servals have water-resistant fur and often lie in shallow water to stalk passing prey. They are solitary hunters, most active from dusk through the night.
our_animals.caracals_detail: Known as the desert lynx, the caracal is the largest of Africa's small cats. Long, powerful legs let it leap nearly 3 metres straight up to knock birds clean out of the air, and it climbs trees with ease. The long black tufts on its ears — used to communicate with other caracals — give it a passing resemblance to a lynx, though it's actually more closely related to servals and golden cats. Caracals are solitary, mostly hunting at twilight, and are rarely seen far from cover.
our_animals.civets_detail: The African civet is easy to recognise by its bold black-and-white spots, stripes and the bandit-like mask around its eyes. Solitary and intensely territorial, it marks its range using scent glands that produce a strong musk. Unlike most mammals, civets have five non-retractable claws on each foot, which helps them climb. Mostly nocturnal, they're omnivores that will eat anything from fruit and insects to small rodents, birds and eggs.
our_animals.porcupines_detail: Africa's largest porcupine species is covered in long, hollow quills that rattle loudly in warning when shaken — and form a dramatic crest along the head and neck when raised. Porcupines are largely monogamous, keeping the same mate for life, but forage for food alone after dark and shelter together in multi-chambered underground dens. Despite their fearsome defences, they're gentle herbivores, feeding on bark, roots, fallen fruit and the occasional crop raid.
our_animals.bat_eared_foxes_detail: This small African fox is unmistakable thanks to ears that can grow over 5 inches tall — built to keep the fox cool and to pick up the faintest rustle of insects underground. Bat-eared foxes are grey with black markings on the face, legs and tail, and their strong digging claws are used to build the underground dens and tunnel networks they live in. They live and forage together in pairs or small family groups, feeding mostly on termites, beetles and other insects through the night.
our_animals.jackals_detail: Highly adaptable and opportunistic, jackals will hunt alone or in pairs just as readily as they'll scavenge a free meal. Black-backed and side-striped jackals are both found across southern Africa, identifiable by the dark saddle of fur running down the back. They're monogamous for life, with bonded pairs marking and defending a shared territory together, and are best known for their sharp, far-carrying howls — used to keep in contact with their mate and warn off rivals at dusk and through the night.
our_animals.cows_detail: Our cattle come from backgrounds of neglect or abandonment, often arriving underweight or in poor health. Once assessed and treated by our veterinary team, they're slowly nursed back to full health and given the run of open grazing land on the reserve. Naturally calm and social, they form small, settled herds and are a familiar, reassuring presence for the volunteers and staff who care for them every day.
our_animals.pets_detail: Alongside the wildlife, Nova's Legacy is also home to a small family of rescued cats and dogs, many of whom arrived as strays or were surrendered by owners who could no longer care for them. They live safely on the property, vaccinated, sterilised and well fed, often becoming the first friendly faces volunteers meet on their morning rounds. Each has its own personality and place in the daily life of the reserve.
our_animals.birds_detail: The reserve's bushveld habitat supports a striking variety of birdlife, from birds of prey patrolling the skies above the enclosures to small, colourful songbirds darting through the acacia trees below. Some are year-round residents, while others pass through on seasonal migration, drawn by the same water sources and open grassland that sustain the reserve's larger animals. For many visitors, the birdlife is an unexpected highlight of every walk through the bush.
our_animals.free_roaming_animals_detail: Across the reserve's open grassland, giraffes, zebras, antelope and other herbivores roam freely, exactly as they would in the wild. Left largely undisturbed, these free-roaming species play a vital role in keeping the bushveld ecosystem healthy — grazing keeps grasses in balance, while their presence supports the same natural food web that has sustained Waterberg's wildlife for generations. For many guests, spotting a herd moving quietly through the bush is one of the most memorable parts of a visit.

ourAnimals.name: Nova
ourAnimals.bio: Nova was the very first cheetah Kim hand-reared, born with one back leg never fully formed. He lived a full, joyful life on three legs and inspired the project that carries his name.

ourAnimals.name: Jagger
ourAnimals.bio: Jagger was a fragile two-month-old cub with multiple fractures when he came into our care. Hand-raised back to full health, he's now a playful, sweet-natured cheetah with one quirk: he loves to chase anything red.

ourAnimals.name: Spirit
ourAnimals.bio: Spirit arrived undernourished and distrustful of people. Months of patient care later, he trots the reserve every morning like he owns it.

ourAnimals.name: Shira
ourAnimals.bio: Shira was rescued as an orphaned cub and raised by the team. She now lives peacefully in the reserve, still curious about every visitor who passes her enclosure.

ourAnimals.name: Bliss & Thunder
ourAnimals.bio: Bliss, a rare white lioness, and Thunder, hand-raised after losing his mother, found each other at Nova's Legacy. Today they roam together as parents of four cubs, and Thunder's roar still echoes across the reserve every evening.

ourAnimals.name: Quake
ourAnimals.bio: Quake came to us at just two weeks old after a breeder couldn't export him. He loves a swim in his pool and never misses the chance for a friendly "tiger hello" at the fence, especially when his partner Storm is close by.

ourAnimals.name: Serval
ourAnimals.bio: Our serval prowls the reserve by night, using its oversized ears to track prey hidden in the grass.

ourAnimals.name: Young Serval
ourAnimals.bio: Born on the reserve, this young serval already shows the oversized ears and long legs that make the species such gifted hunters of birds and rodents.

ourAnimals.name: Caracal
ourAnimals.bio: Our caracal is a shy, solitary hunter, most active at dusk and rarely seen far from the cover of tall grass.

ourAnimals.name: African Civet
ourAnimals.bio: Solitary and mostly active after dark, our African civet is recognisable by its black-and-white markings and the bandit-like mask around its eyes.

ourAnimals.name: Spike
ourAnimals.bio: Spike has called Nova's Legacy home since 2016. This gentle porcupine loves fresh corn and a good scratch through the fence, though her quills mean visitors must always move slowly and calmly around her.

ourAnimals.name: Sandy
ourAnimals.bio: Sandy is one of our bat-eared foxes — small, big-eared, and always digging for insects after dark.

ourAnimals.name: Rescued Cattle
ourAnimals.bio: Rescued from neglect, our cattle graze peacefully across the reserve, part of the wider Nova's Legacy family of animals in our care.

ourAnimals.name: Sanctuary Pets
ourAnimals.bio: Rescued cats and dogs share a safe home at Nova's Legacy, often the first friendly faces volunteers meet each morning on the reserve.

ourAnimals.name: Resident Birds
ourAnimals.bio: From birds of prey patrolling the skies to colourful songbirds in the bush, the reserve's skies are as alive as the ground below.

ourAnimals.name: Tumelo
ourAnimals.bio: Tumelo wanders freely across the reserve, often spotted grazing among the cheetah enclosures at golden hour.

ourAnimals.name: Ghost Pack
ourAnimals.bio: Our pack of African wild dogs roams together as a tight-knit family — playful, noisy and never far from one another.
